title: 边缘的ASIC

note:
《连接》探讨了另一种变化观（该系列的副标题），它拒绝了传统的线性和目的论上的历史进程观。伯克认为，不能孤立地考虑现代世界任何特定部分的发展。
 相反，现代世界的整个格式像是一系列相互联系的事件的结果，每个事件都是由一个人或一个团体出于自身动机（例如，利润，好奇心，宗教信仰）而行动，而没有最终目的的概念，他们或他们当代人的行动最终导致的现代结果。这些孤立事件的结果之间的相互作用是推动历史和创新的动力，也是本系列及其续集的重点。
 为了证明这一观点，伯克在每个情节中都以过去（通常是古代或中世纪）的特定事件或创新作为开端，并通过一系列看似无关的联系追溯到该事件的路径，直至现代世界的基本要素。例如，长链情节追溯了Fluyt（一种荷兰货船）的发展，从而发明了塑料。
    1.触发效应（Trigger Effect）通过对纽约市的详细叙述和1965年的停电事件详细描述了当今世界对复杂技术网络的依赖性。
 2.《早晨的死亡》研究了古代世界中用试金石对贵金属的标准化。
 3.遥远的声音表明电信之所以存在，是因为诺曼人骑马时有马stir，这反过来又使他们在战争中取得了进一步的进步。
 4.《数字信仰》从商业主义，气候变化和黑死病如何影响文化发展的角度考察了从中世纪到文艺复兴的过渡。
5.《命运之轮》追溯了古希腊手稿中的占星学知识，这些手稿来自巴格达的创始人哈里发·曼苏尔（Caliph Al-Mansur），经过甘德沙布尔的穆斯林修道院/医学院，到中世纪教堂对闹钟的需求（水钟楼和濒临流离失所的时钟） ）。
 6.天空中的雷声在烟囱的发明，编织，纽扣，护墙板，墙壁挂毯，墙壁抹灰，玻璃窗以及隐私保护的实践中牵涉了小冰河时代（约公元1250-1300年）。睡觉和做爱。
 7.长链追溯了1500年代荷兰的Fluyt货机的发明。如果船体上覆盖着来自殖民地的沥青和焦油，直到1776年美国独立战争，爱德华·劳埃德（伦敦的劳埃德船级社）都会为航行提供保险。
 8.“吃喝玩乐”首先从塑料，塑料信用卡开始，然后信贷概念又回到了勃艮第公爵，这是第一个使用信贷的州。
 9.倒计时将电影放映机的发明与因发明和使用大炮而导致的城堡防御工事的改善联系起来。
 10.昨天，明天和你。回顾一下：变化会引起更多变化。从犁开始，您会得到工匠，文明，灌溉，陶器和文字，数学，可以预测洪水，帝国的日历以及一个变化如此迅速以至无法跟上的现代世界。
   1.革命-所有这些事物的共同点是：3个祖父的一生，两次革命，1750个康沃尔锡矿，矿井中的水，泵，蒸汽机，瓦特的复印机，复写纸，火柴，磷肥，火车和基因库混合...
2.感伤之旅-它们有什么共同点：弗洛伊德，生活方式危机，电击疗法，催眠疗法，磁性，摩擦学，心理学，生理学，合成染料，本生灯，吸收，弗劳恩霍夫谱系，天文望远镜，色差和调查？
 3.齐心协力-詹姆斯·伯克（James Burke）解释了热气球与笑气之间的关系，并继续进行手术，液压水花园，液压柱塞，穿越阿尔卑斯山的隧道，东方快车，硝酸甘油，心脏病和头痛，阿司匹林，石碳酸，消毒剂，戴姆勒-梅赛德斯（Mabach-Gottlieb Daimler-Mercedes），化油器和直升机。
 4. Whodunit？ -此情节以台球开始，以台球结束。在此过程中，伯克考察了乔治·阿格里科拉（Georgius Agricola）的《论金属》（De Re Metallica），采矿如何支持战争，金钱的作用，西班牙舰队，大型船只，木材短缺，玻璃制造，煤炭，平板玻璃，镜子，六分仪，气压计等问题。 ...
 5.一无所有-穿梭飞机着陆是如何从教会禁止的真空开始的？伯克带领我们进行气压计，天气预报，泥泞和柏油路，雨水径流，污水，霍乱流行，卫生，水暖，陶瓷，真空泵，压缩空气钻机，阿尔卑斯山的隧道...
 6.过去的回声-在这种情况下，过去的历史从荷兰统治的印度的茶开始，考察了日本的茶道，禅宗佛教，瓷器，佛罗伦萨的建筑，代尔夫特陶器，韦奇伍德，免费泥匠，秘密密码，电台-电话，宇宙背景辐射以及-最后是射电天文学。
 7.照片完成-伯克研究的另一项发现，包括伊士曼的电影《柯达布朗尼》，1867年消失的大象恐慌症，台球，赛璐as代替象牙，爆炸的假牙，枪棉，子弹的双发声，马赫冲击波，空气动力学，核弹...
 8.分开的方式-伯克展示了如何通过两条完全独立的途径从糖类转化为原子武器。第一个涉及非洲奴隶，废奴主义社会，桑普森·劳埃德二世，铁丝网，吊桥，镀锌铁丝网，荒野西部定居点，铁丝网，玉米罐头和镉。
9.高峰期-聚乙烯和大本钟之间的联系是分开的，所以让我们重新叙述一下：聚乙烯，雷达，肥皂，人造染料，颜色感知，挂毯，远东商品，假漆家具，寻找更短的路线到日本，格陵兰的哈德逊，发现大量鲸鱼...
 10. Deja Vu-James Burke通过检查黑白电影，征服者，秘鲁印加人，小痘病毒，看起来像西班牙城市的定居点之类的东西，证明了历史的确重演了自己，黄金的丰富度最终出现在比利时，安特卫普，殖民地剥削，埋藏宝物以防止海盗的行为...
 11.新和谐–从微芯片到新加坡，从晶体管到其最重要的元素锗，明花瓶和钴假冒，都实现了乌托邦的梦想，这些都有助于在特殊的伊斯兰场所和马赛克中使用蓝色瓷砖中的蓝色在拜占庭，君士坦丁的捐赠，葡萄牙的星空航行...
 12.辣酱菜-伯克（Burke）始于伊斯坦布尔的香料市场，在那里您可以找到辣酱菜，讲述土耳其人1453年对伊斯坦布尔的重新占领，跟随胡椒，茶和鸦片的踪迹，以及对成瘾者的剥削， Java的丛林，然后到动物园，使用金丝雀作为一氧化碳探测器...
 13.大旋转-基本上是赌博的加利福尼亚彩票。伯克（Burke）从这里开始带我们了解亚历山大·弗莱明（Alexander Flemming）偶然发现青霉素，维耶肖夫特（Vierschoft）的观察结果，即被污染的水与健康有关，席列曼（Schliemann）搜寻特洛伊城，盗窃发现的珍宝以及维耶肖夫特的犯罪学。
 14.绝妙的主意-杜松子酒和滋补品的发明是为了在像Java这样的英国殖民地抗击疟疾，这使我们来到了日内瓦，那里的清洁令人着迷。在这里，用一次性瓶盖密封滋补水，而剃须刀也变得一次性使用，这使我们找到了亨斯迈的钢，这对于制造钟表弹簧和计时码表来说是无价的。
 15.掀起波浪-卷发器帮助女性永久性挥动波浪，这使我们得以探索硼砂，带我们前往瑞士，约翰·萨特（Johan Sutter）的骗局和锯木厂，这意味着发现了通往1848年的黄金加州淘金热。
16.路线-生病的英国律师Jethro Tull调养饮的葡萄酒，并提供help头以帮助解决农业问题。法国的农业生产也不是很好。
 17.一个字-改变一切的一个字是“ filioque”，但我们必须去君士坦丁堡旅行，参观文艺复兴时期，与威尼斯的Aldus Manutius会面，探索缩写，了解斜体印刷，这导致书籍过多，需要开发目录系统。
 18.在此处签名-墨菲定律说，您需要伦敦劳埃德保险公司（Lloyd's of London）的保险，所以请打包行李学习国际法，并通过计算概率来保护自己免受盗版。您最好为此学习Pascal的数学，但是您可能会发现自己因自由思考而入狱。
 19.比真实事物要好-从1890年代开始生产自行车和灯笼裤，然后介绍靴子，拉链，缝纫机以及无穷的差异。细说，我们看的是微观细菌，偏振光，糖，煤，铁，微气泡，分光镜，夜视镜...
 20.弹性回应-异想天开地看一下英国长弓，罗宾汉，绵羊，需要用风车排空土地，复利的影响，小数分数，生产率提高，伊利运河，铁路，电报，百货商店，桂格燕麦，X射线诊断...
   1.反馈-互联网和战时枪支上的电子代理人首先使用克劳德·伯纳德（Claude Bernard）发现的反馈技术，克洛德·伯纳德（Claude Bernard）的活体解剖实验启动了被称为人道社会的动物权利运动，真正的开始是救生艇船员从所有沉船事故中解救了人们，那里所有额外的船只中...
 2.名称是什么？ -还记得上一集中的玉米片吗？由于玉米芯成为粘合剂，将金刚砂（也称为碳化硅）粘合到用于研磨灯泡的砂轮上。
3.放下苹果-在史密森尼学会，我们学习了有助于Pierre和Marie Curie发现镭的电晶体，然后Langevin使用压电晶体开发了声纳，该声纳有助于拯救自由船（来自德国的U型艇） ）结合使用碳弧制成的乙炔的焊接技术...
 4.一个看不见的物体-哈勃望远镜将太空中的黑洞与肼燃料一起带入太空，肼燃料是杀菌葡萄树的副产品，由检疫公约和汇票，美国运通卡和布法罗·比尔，杂技演员提供和法国战役，圣女贞德和宗教裁判所...
 5.生活无野炊-速溶咖啡在第二次世界大战中破土动工，吉普车导致卢迪特人捣破尼龙和放养机，后者由拜伦（Byron）捍卫，拜伦在土耳其与约翰·加尔特（John Galt）会面，避免了同样的障碍激发了“星条旗永不落，”...
 6.基本资料-阿尔弗雷德·罗素·华莱士（Alfred Russel Wallace），他研究了甲虫，奥利弗·约瑟夫·洛奇（Oliver Joseph Lodge）和电报，这是由雷金纳德·费森登（Reginald Fessenden）设计的无线电，供香蕉种植者使用，奥古斯丁·皮拉姆斯·德·坎多尔（Augustin Pyramus de Candolle）研究了无线电，后者使瑞士人在明信片上使用了邮票。哥特式国会大厦的卡通作品，其灵感又来自约翰·戈特弗里德·赫德的浪漫主义运动...
 7.一个特殊的地方-英格兰莱斯特大学的Alec Jeffries爵士教授开发了DNA轮廓分析和schlieren摄影，西奥多·冯·卡曼（Theodore von Karman）将其用于研究空气动力学和安东尼·福克（Anthony Fokker）的机枪以及从意大利开始的红色男爵和地理和浪漫主义思想。 。
 8.天上的火-得益于大陆漂移和阿尔弗雷德·韦格纳（Alfred Wegener）对海市the楼的热情，亚瑟王姐姐的魔幻影像被认为触发了中世纪对通奸的宫廷爱情回答，而这反过来又受到了自由恋爱观念的启发。住在神秘的阴谋家旁边的神秘的Cathars ...
 9.命中注水-多亏了用棕榈油制成的凝固汽油油，也用于人造黄油，并用硅藻土加筋处理，这种工艺来自浮游生物，其生活在巴洛特研究的洋流中，然后观察多普勒效应使菲索测量光速。速度。 Fizeau的岳父的朋友ProsperMérimée撰写了《 Carmen》 ...
10.接触中-从昂内斯发现的使用超导性的廉价聚变能量的尝试开始，与凯利莱特提供的液态气体一起，凯尔莱特在埃菲尔铁塔上进行了实验，埃菲尔铁塔还建造了以其著名的自由女神像犹太活动家艾玛·拉撒路（Emma Lazarus）的诗...
note_en:
Connections explores an  Alternative View of Change (the subtitle of the series) that rejects the conventional linear and teleological view of historical progress. Burke contends that one cannot consider the development of any particular piece of the modern world in isolation.
 Rather, the entire gestalt of the modern world is the result of a web of interconnected events, each one consisting of a person or group acting for reasons of their own (e.g., profit, curiosity, religious) motivations with no concept of the final, modern result of what either their or their contemporaries' actions finally led to. The interplay of the results of these isolated events is what drives history and innovation, and is also the main focus of the series and its sequels.
 To demonstrate this view, Burke begins each episode with a particular event or innovation in the past (usually Ancient or Medieval times) and traces the path from that event through a series of seemingly unrelated connections to a fundamental and essential aspect of the modern world. For example,  The Long Chain episode traces the invention of plastics from the development of the fluyt, a type of Dutch cargo ship.
    1. The Trigger Effect details the world’s present dependence on complex technological networks through a detailed narrative of New York City and the power blackout of 1965.
 2. Death in the Morning examines the standardization of precious metal with the touchstone in the ancient world.
 3. Distant Voices suggests that telecommunications exist because Normans had stirrups for horse riding which in turn led them to further advancements in warfare.
 4. Faith in Numbers examines the transition from the Middle Ages to the Renaissance from the perspective of how commercialism, climate change and the Black Death influenced cultural development.
 5. The Wheel of Fortune traces astrological knowledge in ancient Greek manuscripts from Baghdad’s founder, Caliph Al-Mansur, via the Muslim monastery/medical school at Gundeshapur, to the medieval Church’s need for alarm clocks (the water horologium and the verge and foliot clock).
 6. Thunder in the Skies implicates the Little Ice Age (ca. 1250-1300 AD) in the invention of the chimney, as well as knitting, buttons, wainscoting, wall tapestries, wall plastering, glass windows, and the practice of privacy for sleeping and sex.
 7. The Long Chain traces the invention of the Fluyt freighter in Holland in the 1500s. Voyages were insured by Edward Lloyd (Lloyd's of London) if the ships hulls were covered in pitch and tar which came from the colonies until the American Revolution in 1776.
 8. Eat, Drink and Be Merry begins with plastic, the plastic credit card and the concept of credit then leaps back in time to to the Dukes of Burgundy, which was the first state to use credit.
 9. Countdown connects the invention of the movie projector to improvements in castle fortifications caused by the invention and use of the cannon.
 10. Yesterday, Tomorrow and You. A bit of a recap: change causes more change. Start with the plow, you get craftsmen, civilization, irrigation, pottery and writing, mathematics, a calendar to predict floods, empires, and a modern world where change happens so rapidly you can't keep up.
   1. Revolutions - What do all these things have in common: 3 grandfathers' lifetimes, 2 revolutions, 1750 Cornwall tin mines, water in mines, pumps, steam engines, Watt's copier, carbon paper, matches, phosphorus fertilizer, trains and gene pool mixing...
 2. Sentimental Journeys - What do these have in common: Freud, lifestyle crisis, electric shock therapy, hypnotherapy, magnetism, frenology, penology, physiology, synthetic dyes, the Bunsen burner, absorption, Fraunhofer lines, astronomical telescopes, chromatic aberrations, and surveying?
 3. Getting it Together - James Burke explains the relationship between hot air balloons and laughing gas, and goes on to surgery, hydraulic water gardens, hydraulic rams, tunneling through the Alps, the Orient Express, nitroglycerin, heart attacks & headaches, aspirin, carbolic acid, disinfectant, Mabach-Gottlieb Daimler-Mercedes, carburetors, and helicopters.
 4. Whodunit? - This episode starts with a billiard ball and ends with a billiard ball. Along the way, Burke examines Georgius Agricola's De Re Metallica, how mining supported war, the role of money, the Spanish Armada, large ships, problems posed by a wood shortage, glass making, coal, plate glass, mirrors, the sextant, barometers...
 5. Something for Nothing - How do shuttle landings start with the vacuum which was forbidden by the Church? Burke takes us on an adventure with barometers, weather forecasting, muddy and blacktop roads, rain runoff, sewage, a cholera epidemic, hygiene, plumbing, ceramics, vacuum pumps, compressed air drills, tunnels in the Alps...
 6. Echoes of the Past - The past in this case starts with the tea in Dutch-ruled India, examines the Japanese tea ceremony, Zen Buddhism, porcelain, the architecture of Florence, Delftware, Wedgwood, Free Masons, secret codes, radio-telephones, cosmic background radiation and - finally – radio astronomy.
 7. Photo Finish - Another series of discoveries examined by Burke which include Eastman's film Kodak Brownie, the disappearing elephant scare of 1867, billiard balls, celluloid as a substitute for ivory, false teeth that explode, gun cotton, double shot sound of a bullet, Mach's shock wave, aerodynamics, nuclear bombs...
 8. Separate Ways - Burke shows how to get from sugar to atomic weapons by two totally independent paths. The first involves African slaves, Abolitionist societies, Sampson Lloyd II, wire, suspension bridges, galvanized wire, settlement of the wild West, barbed wire, canned corn, and cadmium.
 9. High Times - The connection between polyethylene and Big Ben is a few degrees of separation, so let's recount them: polyethylene, radar, soap, artificial dyes, color perception, tapestries, far East goods, fake lacquer furniture, search for shorter route to Japan, Hudson in Greenland, the discovery of plentiful whales...
 10. Deja Vu - James Burke provides evidence that history does repeat itself by examining the likes of black and white movies, Conquistadors, Peruvian Incas, small pox, settlements that look like Spain's cities, the gold abundance ending up in Belgium, Antwerp, colony exploitation, the practice of burying treasure to avoid pirates...
 11. New Harmony - A dream of utopia is followed from microchips to Singapore, from the transistor to its most important element, germanium, to Ming Vases and cobalt fakes, which contribute to the blue in blue tiles used in special Islamic places, and Mosaics in Byzantium, the donation of Constantine, Portuguese navigation by stars...
 12. Hot Pickle - Burke starts out in a spice market in Istanbul where you can find hot pickle, recounts the retaking of Istanbul by the Turks in 1453, follows the trail of pepper and tea and opium and the exploitation of addicts, moves to the jungles of Java, then to zoos, the use of canaries as carbon monoxide detectors...
 13. The Big Spin - is a California lottery which is basically gambling. From here Burke takes us through Alexander Flemming's chance discovery of penicillin, to Vierschoft's observation that contaminated water is related to health, to Schliemann's search for City of Troy, the theft of discovered treasure, and to Vierschoft's criminology.
 14. Bright Ideas - Gin and tonic was invented to combat Malaria in British colonies like Java, which leads us to Geneva where cleanliness is an obsession. Here tonic water was sealed with a disposable bottle cap, and razors became disposable, leading us to Huntsman's steel, invaluable for making clock springs and chronometers.
 15. Making Waves - a permanent wave in ladies' hair is aided by curlers, and this leads us to explore borax, taking us to Switzerland, Johan Sutter's scam, and the saw mill, and that means the discovery of gold leading to the 1848 California gold rush.
 16. Routes - Jethro Tull, a sick English lawyer, recuperates sipping wine and contributes the hoe to help fix farming problems. Farm production is not going so well in France, either.
 17. One Word - The one word that changed everything was "filioque" but we must make a trip to Constantinople, visit the Renaissance, meet Aldus Manutius of Venice, explore abbreviations, learn about Italic print, which resulted in an overload of books, requiring the development of a cataloging system.
 18. Sign Here - Murphy's Law says you need insurance from Lloyd's of London, so pack your bags to study international law and protect yourself from piracy by calculating the probability. You better study Pascal's math for that, but you might find yourself jailed for free thinking.
 19. Better Than the Real Thing - starts in the 1890's with bicycles and bloomers and then takes a look at boots, zippers, sewing machines, and infinitesimal difference. Speaking of small, we look at microscopic germs, Polarized light, sugar, coal, iron, micro-bubbles, the spectroscope, night vision...
 20. Flexible Response - is a whimsical look at the myth of the English longbow, Robin Hood, sheep, the need to drain land with windmills, the effect of compound interest, decimal fractions, increased productivity, the Erie Canal, railroads, telegraphs, department stores, Quaker Oats, X-ray diagnostics...
   1. Feedback - Electronic agents on the Internet and wartime guns use feedback techniques discovered in the first place by Claude Bernard, whose vivisection experiments kick off animal rights movements called humane societies that really start out as lifeboat crews rescuing people from all the shipwrecks happening because of all the extra ships out there...
 2. What's in a Name? - Remember the cornflakes from last episode? Thanks to the fact that corncobs make adhesives to bond Carborundum, otherwise known as silicon carbide, to grinding wheels used to grind light-bulbs.
 3. Drop the Apple - At the Smithsonian, we learn of electric crystals that help Pierre and Marie Curie discover what they call radium, and then Langevin uses the piezo-electric crystal to develop sonar that helps save liberty ships (from German U-boats) put together with welding techniques using acetylene made with carbon arcs...
 4. An Invisible Object - Black holes in space, seen by the Hubble Telescope, brought into space with hydrazine fuel, which was a by-product of fungicidal French vines, fueled by quarantine conventions and money orders, American Express and Buffalo Bill, Vaudeville and French battles, Joan of Arc and the Inquisition...
 5. Life is No Picnic - Instant coffee gets off the ground in World War II and Jeeps lead to nylons and stocking machines smashed by Luddites, who were defended by Byron, who meets John Galt in Turkey, avoiding the same blockade that inspires the "Star-Spangled Banner,"...
 6. Elementary Stuff - Alfred Russel Wallace, who studied beetles, Oliver Joseph Lodge and telegraphy, a radio designed by Reginald Fessenden, which was used by banana growers, studied by Augustin Pyramus de Candolle, who got the Swiss to use stamps on postcards with cartoons of Gothic Houses of parliament, which in turn had been inspired by Johann Gottfried Herder's Romantic movement...
 7. A Special Place - Professor Sir Alec Jeffries of Leicester University in England develops DNA profiling and schlieren photography used by Theodore von Karman to study aerodynamics and Anthony Fokker's airborne machine guns and the Red Baron and geography and Romantic ideas that start in Italy...
 8. Fire from the Sky - Thanks to Continental Drift and Alfred Wegener's passion for mirages, magic images from the sister of King Arthur, whose chivalry supposedly triggers the medieval courtly love answer to adultery, which were in turn inspired by the free love ideas of the mystical Cathars, who lived next to the mystical cabalists...
 9. Hit the Water - Thanks to napalm, made with palm oil, also used for margarine, stiffened with a process using kieselguhr that comes from plankton living in currents studied by Ballot bbefore observing the Doppler Effect that caused Fizeau to measure the speed of light speed. Fizeau's father-in-law's friend, Prosper Mérimée, who wrote "Carmen"...
 10. In Touch - Starting from an attempt for cheaper fusion power using superconductivity, which was discovered by Onnes, with liquid gas provided by Cailletet, who carried out experiments on a tower built by Eiffel, who also built the Statue of Liberty with its famous poem by the Jewish activist Emma Lazarus...
title: 詹姆斯·伯克（1978）

note:
沙特阿拉伯正在红海沙漠中建造一个名为Neom的未来城市。这个耗资5000亿美元的城市-包括出租车和机器人家庭助教，正在从头开始建造，并将有100万人居住。什么样的能源产品既可以用来为这座城市供电，又可以卖给全世界？不是油。沙特人正在大力发展一种叫做“绿色氢”的物质。绿色氢是一种无碳燃料，它是通过使用可再生发电来将氢分子从氧分子中分离出来的，由水制成的。
 今年夏天，一家大型美国天然气公司Air Products＆Chemicals宣布，作为Neom的一部分，过去四年来它一直在沙特阿拉伯建造一座绿色的氢气工厂。该工厂由遍布沙漠的风力发电和太阳能发电项目提供的4吉瓦电力驱动。它声称是世界上最大的绿色氢项目-越来越多的沙特工厂正在筹划中。
 绿氢？并非只有沙特人相信这是能源未来的下一件大事。尽管在美国，这种燃料几乎没有受到关注，但在世界范围内，绿色的氢热浪仍在进行，许多公司，投资者，政府和环保主义者认为，它是一种能源，可以帮助结束化石燃料的统治并减缓世界变暖的轨迹。
 “这是非常有希望的，”自然资源保护委员会的能源分析师雷切尔•法赫里（Rachel Fakhry）说。像法赫里（Fakhry）这样的专家表示，尽管风能和太阳能可以为房屋和电动汽车提供电力，但绿色氢可能是能源密集型行业（如混凝土和钢铁制造业）以及交通运输部门中的理想动力源。更加难以电气化。法赫里在一次采访中说：“航空业，航运，制造业，长途货运很难清理掉最后15％的经济。” “绿色氢可以做到这一点。”
    欧洲的能源价格高昂，并且严重依赖俄罗斯的天然气，欧洲正在通过为电解工厂和其他氢基础设施的建设提供资金来拥抱绿色氢。德国已将其清洁能源刺激资金的最大份额分配给绿色氢。欧盟委员会在七月份的战略文件中写道：“这是完全脱碳的经济之谜的缺失部分。”
 怀疑论者认为，氢作为燃料来源的潜力已经被吹捧了几十年，但是该技术从未在相当大的规模上起步。他们认为，绿色氢技术的广泛采用面临着严重的障碍，最显着的是氢燃料需要可再生能源才能实现绿色，这将需要大规模扩展可再生能源来为将水分解为氢和氧的电解工厂提供动力。没有管道，绿色氢也很难储存和运输。目前，在某些地方（例如美国），氢比其他燃料（例如天然气）贵很多。
 彭博新能源财经（Bloomberg New Energy Finance）分析师，绿色氢怀疑论者迈克尔•利布雷希（Michael Liebreich）表示，尽管它具有优势，但它也显示出同样令人印象深刻的劣势。
Liebreich在最近为BloombergNEF发表的两篇文章中写道：“它在自然界中不会发生，因此需要能量来分离。” “其存储需要压缩至大气压的700倍，制冷至253摄氏度……每单位体积的天然气携带四分之一的能量……它会使金属脆化；它通过最小的泄漏逃逸，是的，它确实具有爆炸性。”
 尽管存在这些问题，Liebreich写道，绿色氢仍然“对技术优化主义者的想象力持有一种恶习般的控制力。”
     Wood McKenzie的能源分析师本·加拉格尔（Ben Gallagher）研究绿色氢气，他说这种燃料太新了，其未来仍不清楚。他说：“没人知道这里发生了什么。” “这是猜测。目前，很难将其视为新油。但是，它可能构成整体燃料组合的重要组成部分。”
 氢是宇宙中最丰富的化学物质。氢的两个原子与氧的原子配对会生成水。但是，氢是一种无味无味的气体，极易燃烧。甲烷产生的氢（通常来自天然气，还有煤炭和生物质）是德国在第二次世界大战中开创的，它没有石油沉积。但是二氧化碳是由甲烷产生的氢气产生的，因此对气候不利。以这种方式制造的氢称为灰色氢。
 绿色是氢块的新成员，因为它是用可再生能源制造的，所以不含二氧化碳。此外，使用可再生能源生产燃料可以帮助解决困扰风能和太阳能的间歇性问题，因此本质上是有效的存储。当对可再生能源的需求较低时，在春季和秋季，可以使用多余的电力来驱动分解氢和氧分子所需的电解。然后，氢气可以存储或沿管道输送。
 这些优势正在激发人们对全球绿色氢的兴趣。在欧洲，中东和亚洲，越来越多的国家和公司开始使用这种高质量的燃料。美国之所以落后，是因为天然气等其他能源的价格要便宜得多，但是一些新项目正在进行中，包括犹他州的绿色氢发电厂，该电厂将取代两座老化的燃煤电厂并为南加州生产电力。 。
    在日本，世界最大的绿色氢能工厂刚刚在福岛附近开业。鉴于该工厂距离2011年核灾难的地点很近，这是一个有意象征性的位置。它将用于为车辆和固定地点的燃料电池供电。澳大利亚的一个能源财团刚刚宣布计划在皮尔巴拉建设一个名为“亚洲可再生能源枢纽”的项目，该项目将使用1,743台大型风力涡轮机和30平方英里的太阳能电池板来运营一个26吉瓦的电解工厂，该工厂将产生绿色氢气，然后发送给新加坡。
随着欧洲加大脱碳力度，它也在燃料上大赌注。欧盟刚刚起草了大规模扩大绿色氢能的战略，尽管该战略尚未被正式采用。但是，在其5500亿美元的清洁能源计划中，欧盟包括用于新型绿色氢电解器以及该燃料的运输和存储技术的资金。欧盟委员会写道：“快速部署清洁氢气是欧盟实现其高气候野心的关键。”
 拥有世界上最便宜的风能和太阳能的中东，正努力成为绿色氢能的主要参与者。落基山学院突破性技术计划负责人托马斯·科克·布兰克（Thomas Koch Blank）说：“沙特阿拉伯拥有可笑的低成本可再生能源。” “每天太阳都非常可靠地发光，而风每天晚上都非常可靠地吹。很难被击败。”
 彭博社（BloombergNEF）估计，要产生足够的绿色氢来满足世界四分之一的能源需求，将消耗的电能比现在从所有来源产生的电能还要多，并且在生产和储存方面的投资为11万亿美元。这就是为什么目前的重点放在经济的15％上，而风能和太阳能等能源的需求不容易由风能和太阳能来满足，例如重型制造，长途货运以及货船和飞机的燃料。
     绿氢的能量密度是喷气燃料的三倍，使其成为一种有希望的飞机零排放技术。但是欧洲飞机制造商空中客车公司（Airbus）最近发表声明说，需要解决的重大问题包括安全地将氢气储存在飞机上，机场缺乏氢气基础设施以及成本。专家说，将需要新技术来解决这些问题。不过，空中客车公司认为绿色氢将在航空运输的脱碳中发挥重要作用。
 空中客车零排放飞机副总裁Glen Llewellyn表示：“为使零排放飞行成为现实，必须建立具有成本竞争力的绿色氢气和跨行业的合作伙伴关系。”他说，氢动力飞机可能会在2035年之前飞行。
 在美国，能源价格低廉，绿色氢气的成本大约是天然气的三倍，尽管这一价格并未考虑到化石燃料对环境的破坏。但是，绿色氢的价格正在下降。在10年内，绿色氢的成本可望与美国的天然气相当。
 美国推动绿色氢发展的主要动力是加利福尼亚向碳中和未来迈进的积极努力。例如，洛杉矶水电局正在帮助资助犹他州绿色氢燃料发电厂的建设。它计划于2025年上线。
一家名为SGH2的公司最近宣布，它将在加利福尼亚南部建造一座大型工厂，生产绿色氢气。但是，它不使用电解，而是使用废物气化，将多种类型的废物加热至高温，从而将其还原为分子化合物。这些分子然后与氢结合，SGH2声称它可以比使用电解更便宜地制造绿色氢。
    加州官员还认为，绿色氢可替代柴油车辆的化石燃料。该州于2009年通过了《低碳燃料标准》，以推广电动汽车和氢能汽车。上个月，一群重型汽车和能源行业官员组建了西方国家氢联盟，敦促迅速部署氢燃料电池技术和基础设施，以取代柴油卡车，公共汽车，机车和飞机。
 西方国家氢能联盟执行董事Roxana Bekemohammadi表示：“氢燃料电池将为这些重型，难以电气化的部门的零排放机动性提供动力。” “这一事实无可争辩。这个新联盟的存在是为了确保政府和企业可以有效地合作，以加速这场革命的到来。”
 今年早些时候，美国能源部宣布投资1亿美元，以帮助开发大型，价格合理的电解器，并为长途卡车开发新的燃料电池技术。
 在澳大利亚，新南威尔士大学与一家全球工程公司GHD合作，创建了一个名为LAVO的家用系统，该系统使用太阳能在家用系统中生成和存储绿色氢。氢气根据需要转换回电能。
     落基山研究所的布兰克说，所有这些发展都是“真正的好消息。由于气候变化问题似乎无法解决，因此绿色氢具有解决许多使人们彻夜难眠的事物的巨大潜力。”
note_en:
Saudi Arabia is constructing a futuristic city in the desert on the Red Sea called Neom. The $500 billion city — complete with flying taxis and robotic domestic help — is being built from scratch and will be home to a million people. And what energy product will be used both to power this city and sell to the world? Not oil. The Saudis are going big on something called green hydrogen — a carbon-free fuel made from water by using renewably produced electricity to split hydrogen molecules from oxygen molecules.
 This summer, a large U.S. gas company, Air Products & Chemicals, announced that as part of Neom it has been building a green hydrogen plant in Saudi Arabia for the last four years. The plant is powered by 4 gigawatts from wind and solar projects that sprawl across the desert. It claims to be the world’s largest green hydrogen project — and more Saudi plants are on the drawing board.
 Green hydrogen? The Saudis aren’t alone in believing it’s the next big thing in the energy future. While the fuel is barely on the radar in the United States, around the world a green hydrogen rush is underway, and many companies, investors, governments, and environmentalists believe it is an energy source that could help end the reign of fossil fuels and slow the world’s warming trajectory.
 “It is very promising,” said Rachel Fakhry, an energy analyst for the Natural Resources Defense Council. Experts like Fakhry say that while wind and solar energy can provide the electricity to power homes and electric cars, green hydrogen could be an ideal power source for energy-intensive industries like concrete and steel manufacturing, as well as parts of the transportation sector that are more difficult to electrify. “The last 15 percent of the economy is hard to clean up — aviation, shipping, manufacturing, long-distance trucking,” Fakhry said in an interview. “Green hydrogen can do that.”
    Europe, which has an economy that is saddled with high energy prices and is heavily dependent on Russian natural gas, is embracing green hydrogen by providing funding for construction of electrolysis plants and other hydrogen infrastructure. Germany has allocated  the largest share of its clean energy stimulus funds to green hydrogen. “It is the missing part of the puzzle to a fully decarbonized economy,” the European Commission wrote in a July strategy document.
 Hydrogen’s potential as a fuel source has been touted for decades, but the technology has never gotten off the ground on a sizeable scale — and with good reason, according to skeptics. They argue that widespread adoption of green hydrogen technologies has faced serious obstacles, most notably that hydrogen fuels need renewable energy to be green, which will require a massive expansion of renewable generation to power the electrolysis plants that split water into hydrogen and oxygen. Green hydrogen is also hard to store and transport without a pipeline. And right now in some places, such as the U.S., hydrogen is a lot more expensive than other fuels such as natural gas.
 While it has advantages, says Michael Liebreich, a Bloomberg New Energy Finance analyst in the United Kingdom and a green hydrogen skeptic, “it displays an equally impressive list of disadvantages.”
 “It does not occur in nature so it requires energy to separate,” Liebreich wrote in a pair of  recent essays for BloombergNEF. “Its storage requires compression to 700 times atmospheric pressure, refrigeration to 253 degrees Celsius… It carries one quarter the energy per unit volume of natural gas… It can embrittle metal; it escapes through the tiniest leaks and yes, it really is explosive.”
 In spite of these problems, Liebreich wrote, green hydrogen still “holds a vice-like grip over the imaginations of techno-optimists.”
     Ben Gallagher, an energy analyst at Wood McKenzie who studies green hydrogen, said the fuel is so new that its future remains unclear. “No one has any true idea what is going on here,” he said. “It’s speculation at this point. Right now it’s difficult to view this as the new oil. However, it could make up an important part of the overall fuel mix.”
 Hydrogen is the most abundant chemical in the universe. Two atoms of hydrogen paired with an atom of oxygen creates water. Alone, though, hydrogen is an odorless and tasteless gas, and highly combustible. Hydrogen derived from methane — usually from natural gas, but also coal and biomass — was pioneered in World War II by Germany, which has no petroleum deposits. But CO2 is emitted in manufacturing hydrogen from methane and so it’s not climate friendly; hydrogen manufactured this way is known as gray hydrogen.
 Green is the new kid on the hydrogen block, and because it’s manufactured with renewable energy, it’s CO2-free. Moreover, using renewable energy to create the fuel can help solve the problem of intermittency that plagues wind and solar power, and so it is essentially efficient storage. When demand for renewables is low, during the spring and fall, excess electricity can be used to power the electrolysis that is needed to split hydrogen and oxygen molecules. Then the hydrogen can be stored or sent down a pipeline.
 Such advantages are fueling growing interest in global green hydrogen. Across Europe, the Middle East, and Asia, more countries and companies are embracing this high-quality fuel. The U.S. lags behind because other forms of energy, such as natural gas, are much cheaper, but several new projects are getting underway, including a  green hydrogen power plant in Utah that will replace two aging coal-fired plants and produce electricity for southern California.
    In Japan, a new green hydrogen plant, one of the world’s largest, just opened near Fukishima — an intentionally symbolic location given the plant’s proximity to the site of the 2011 nuclear disaster. It will be used to power fuel cells, both in vehicles and at stationary sites. An energy consortium in Australia just announced plans to build a project called the Asian Renewable Energy Hub in Pilbara that would use 1,743 large wind turbines and 30 square miles of solar panels to run a 26-gigawatt electrolysis factory that would create green hydrogen to send to Singapore.
 As Europe intensifies its decarbonization drive, it, too, is betting big on the fuel. The European Union just drafted a strategy for a large-scale green hydrogen expansion, though it hasn’t been officially adopted yet. But in its $550-billion clean energy plan, the EU is including funds for new green hydrogen electrolyzers and transport and storage technology for the fuel. “Large-scale deployment of clean hydrogen at a fast pace is key for the EU to achieve its high climate ambitions,” the European Commission wrote.
 The Middle East, which has the world’s cheapest wind and solar power, is angling to be a major player in green hydrogen. “Saudi Arabia has ridiculously low-cost renewable power,” said Thomas Koch Blank, leader of the Rocky Mountain Institute’s Breakthrough Technology Program. “The sun is shining pretty reliably every day and the wind is blowing pretty reliably every night. It’s hard to beat.”
 BloombergNEF estimates that to generate enough green hydrogen to meet a quarter of the world’s energy needs would take more electricity than the world generates now from all sources and an investment of $11 trillion in production and storage. That’s why the focus for now is on the 15 percent of the economy with energy needs not easily supplied by wind and solar power, such as heavy manufacturing, long-distance trucking, and fuel for cargo ships and aircraft.
     The energy density of green hydrogen is three times that of jet fuel, making it a promising zero-emissions technology for aircraft. But Airbus, the European airplane manufacturer, recently released a  statement saying that significant problems need to be overcome, including safely storing hydrogen on aircraft, the lack of a hydrogen infrastructure at airports, and cost. Experts say that new technologies will be needed to solve these problems. Nevertheless, Airbus believes green hydrogen will play an important role in decarbonizing air transport.
 “Cost-competitive green hydrogen and cross-industry partnerships will be mandatory to bring zero-emission flying to reality,” said Glen Llewellyn, vice president of Zero Emission Aircraft for Airbus. Hydrogen-powered aircraft could be flying by 2035, he said.
 In the U.S., where energy prices are low, green hydrogen costs about three times as much as natural gas, though that price doesn’t factor in the environmental damage caused by fossil fuels. The price of green hydrogen is falling, however. In 10 years, green hydrogen is expected to be comparable in cost to natural gas in the United States.
 A major driver of green hydrogen development in the U.S. is California’s aggressive push toward a carbon-neutral future. The Los Angeles Department of Water and Power, for example, is helping fund the construction of the green hydrogen-fueled power plant in Utah. It’s scheduled to go online in 2025.
 A company called SGH2 recently announced it would build a large facility to produce green hydrogen in southern California. Instead of using electrolysis, though, it will use waste gasification, which heats many types of waste to high temperatures that reduce them to their molecular compounds. Those molecules then bind with hydrogen, and SGH2 claims it can make green hydrogen more cheaply than using electrolysis.
    California officials also see green hydrogen as an alternative to fossil fuels for diesel vehicles. The state passed a Low Carbon Fuel Standard in 2009 to promote electric vehicles and hydrogen vehicles. Last month, a group of heavy-duty vehicle and energy industry officials formed the  Western States Hydrogen Alliance to press for rapid deployment of hydrogen fuel cell technology and infrastructure to replace diesel trucks, buses, locomotives, and aircraft.
 “Hydrogen fuel cells will power the future of zero-emission mobility in these heavy-duty, hard-to-electrify sectors,” said Roxana Bekemohammadi, executive director of the Western States Hydrogen Alliance. “That fact is indisputable. This new alliance exists to ensure government and industry can work efficiently together to accelerate the coming of this revolution.”
 Earlier this year, the U.S. Department of Energy announced a $100 million investment to help develop large, affordable electrolyzers and to create new fuel cell technologies for long-haul trucks.
 In Australia, the University of New South Wales, in partnership with a global engineering firm, GHD, has created a home-based system called LAVO that uses solar energy to generate and store green hydrogen in home systems. The hydrogen is converted back into electricity as needed.
     All these developments, says Blank of the Rocky Mountain Instiute, are “really good news. Green hydrogen has high potential to address many of the things that keep people awake at night because the climate change problem seems unsolvable.”
title: 绿色氢：这对实现无碳经济至关重要吗？
title_en: Green Hydrogen: Could It Be Key to a Carbon-Free Economy?
